1: Chapter 21 addresses priestly holiness specifically — the higher standard required of those who serve at the altar and approach the divine presence. El-hakkohanim benei Aharon: to the priests, the sons of Aaron. The priesthood has additional restrictions beyond those of ordinary Israelites, proportional to their proximity to the holy. The principle is consistent throughout Leviticus: the closer to the divine presence, the stricter the purity requirements.
2: Ki im-lisheero haqarov alav: except for his nearest kin — mother, father, son, daughter, brother. Priests may defile themselves (yitamma = become ritually impure) for these immediate family members by handling their corpses. The permitted exceptions define the irreducible family circle within which even priestly purity requirements yield to the obligations of family love and burial duty. The restriction to immediate relatives makes the list of exceptions minimal and precise.
3: Ulechistoh habetulah haqerovah elaiv asher lo hayta leish lah yittamma: for his sister, a virgin who is near to him and has had no husband, he may become unclean. The unmarried sister (still in the paternal household, not transferred to another family unit) counts among the immediate family for whom a priest may defile himself. The married sister, having joined another household, is no longer counted — a reflection of the patrilineal household structure where marriage transferred a woman's primary familial identity.
4: Lo yitamma ba'al beammav lechallelo: he shall not defile himself as a husband among his people, so as to profane himself. This verse is much disputed — it may refer to an in-law relationship or to a general prohibition against the priest defiling himself for more distant kin beyond the immediate family list.
5: Lo yiqrechu qorcha beroshom ufe'at zeqanam lo yegallechu: they shall not make bald patches on their heads or shave off the edges of their beards or make cuts (seret) on their flesh. The three prohibited mourning practices (head-shaving, beard-trimming, cutting the body) that were prohibited for all Israelites in 19:27-28 are here specifically prohibited for priests. The priest's body must remain unmarked — whole and undamaged — as befits one who represents the wholeness of the covenant community before YHWH.
6: Qedoshim yihyu leiloheichem velo yechalelu shem eloheihem: they shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God. The priest's holiness is specifically connected to the divine name: the priest's actions directly reflect on YHWH's name in a way that ordinary Israelites' actions do not. The priest mediates between the community and God — any defilement of the priest is a profanation of the divine name because the priest represents YHWH to the people and the people to YHWH.
7: Isha zonah vachalala lo yiqachu: they shall not marry a prostitute (zonah) or a woman who has been defiled (chalala = profaned/desecrated, a woman who has lost her ritual-purity status) or a woman divorced from her husband. The marriage restrictions for priests reflect the requirement that the priestly household be free from anything that carries the connotations of defilement or social dishonor. Chalala (the profaned/desecrated woman) is the feminine form of chillel (to profane) — a woman excluded from priestly circles.
8: Veqiddashto ki et-lechem elohecha hu maqriv: you shall sanctify (qiddashto) him, for he offers the bread of your God. The entire community has a stake in priestly holiness: veqiddashto is addressed to Israel collectively. The priestly holiness belongs not to the individual priest alone but to the community that depends on his mediation. YHWH himself is the sanctifier: ki qadosh ani YHWH meqaddishchem (for holy am I, YHWH who sanctifies you).
9: Uvat ish kohen ki techel liznot: and the daughter of a priest, if she profanes herself by whoring (liznot). She shall be burned with fire (va-esh tisref) — the most severe penalty in the chapter. The priest's daughter represents the priestly household's honor in a way that ordinary Israelites' daughters do not. Her sexual misconduct profanes her father: et-aviha hi mechalelet. The burning penalty (also in 20:14) is reserved for the most serious cases in the Holiness Code.
10: Vehakohen haggadol me-echav asher-yutzaq al-rosho shemen hamishcha: the high priest (kohen haggadol, literally the great priest) — upon whose head the anointing oil (shemen hamishcha) has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments. The high priest bears the highest standard of holiness. He shall not uncover his head (lo yifra rosho) or tear his garments (bigadav lo yifrom) — the two standard mourning actions that even ordinary priests are restricted in performing. The high priest is completely prohibited from both.
11: Veal kol-nafshot met lo yavo: he shall not go near any dead body at all. The high priest may not defile himself even for his own father or mother — the strictest possible application of the proximity-holiness principle. The high priest's ongoing ritual purity is so essential to the functioning of the sanctuary (especially on Yom Kippur) that personal bereavement cannot be allowed to disqualify him. This creates an extraordinary human cost: the high priest must refrain from burying his own parents.
12: Umin-hamiqddash lo yetze velo yechalel et-miqdash elohav: he shall not leave the sanctuary (hamiqddash) or profane the sanctuary of his God. The high priest may not leave to attend a funeral even outside the sanctuary precincts. The reason: ki nezer shemen mishchat elohav alav (for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is upon him). Nezer (consecration, crown — also the crown/diadem of the high priest's turban, Lev 8:9) describes the high priest's permanent state of consecration: the anointing oil that marks him as set apart cannot be temporarily set aside for personal mourning.
13: Vehu isha bivtuleiha yiqqach: he shall take a wife in her virginity (bivtuleiha). The high priest's wife must be a virgin (betulah) — an additional restriction beyond the ordinary priest's marriage rules (v7). The requirement of a virgin wife for the high priest reflects the highest level of the purity-and-wholeness principle: the high priest's household must be free from any previous sexual relationship that might carry the associations of mixed loyalty or prior covenant.
14: Almana ugerushah vechalala zonah et-eleh lo yiqqach: a widow, a divorced woman, a desecrated woman, a prostitute — these he shall not marry. The list for the high priest is more extensive than for ordinary priests (v7): widows are also excluded (ordinary priests may marry widows). Only betulat amav (a virgin from his own people) is permitted. The restriction to a virgin from his own people preserves the high priest's household as a symbol of unbroken covenant fidelity.
15: Velo yechalel zaro beammav: he shall not profane his offspring among his people. The high priest's marriage restrictions are motivated by dynastic as well as personal holiness: the priestly line must be maintained in an unambiguous state of holiness. Ki ani YHWH meqaddisho: for I am YHWH who sanctifies him — YHWH himself is the source of the high priest's sanctification, which the marriage rules protect and maintain.
16: YHWH speaks to Moses to deliver the physical-defect regulations for priests who cannot offer the bread of God at the altar.
17: Ish min-zaracha ledorotam asher yihyeh vo mum lo yiqrav lehaqriv lechem elohav: any man of Aaron's descendants throughout their generations who has a defect (mum) may not approach to offer the bread of his God. Mum (defect, blemish, spot) is the same word used for the unblemished quality required of sacrificial animals. The principle of correspondence: as the offering must be without defect, so must the offerer. The priest represents the perfection of what human beings are called to be before God — physical wholeness symbolizes the spiritual and moral integrity required for covenant mediation.
18: The list of disqualifying defects: iver (blind), pisseach (lame), charum (mutilated face/nose — or with a defect of the nose, debated), sarua (a limb too long), shever regel (broken foot), shever yad (broken hand).
19: Gibbein o daq: hunchbacked (gibbein) or dwarfed (daq). The list continues with conditions affecting posture and stature. Tevalul beeino: defect in his eye — perhaps a cataract or other visual impairment beyond full blindness. Garav: itch/scurvy. Yalefet: scabs. Merua ashech: crushed testicle — the final item in the list covers damage to the reproductive system.
20: This verse continues listing the defects (including crushed testicle) from v19. The comprehensive list demonstrates that the restriction covers all categories of physical imperfection — not as a moral judgment but as a symbolic system: those who represent the covenant community before a holy God must embody the completeness and wholeness that the covenant life is designed to produce.
21: Kol-ish asher-bo mum mizera Aharon hakohen lo yiggash lehaqriv et-ishei YHWH: any man of Aaron's descendants who has a defect shall not approach to offer the fire offerings of YHWH. The prohibiton is absolute for altar service, but qualified in the next verse: the defective priest may still eat the sacred food.
22: Lechem elohav miqodshei haqodashim umin-haqodashim yochal: he may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy things and of the holy things. The defective priest retains his status as priest and his right to eat the sacred food; he is only prohibited from active altar service. The distinction preserves his dignity and his covenant standing while maintaining the integrity of the altar service. The defect affects what he may do, not who he is.
23: Ach el-happarochet lo yavo ve-el-hamizbeach lo yiggash: but he shall not go through the veil (parochet) or approach the altar. The two most sacred locations — the veil separating the holy of holies and the altar of burnt offering — are specifically prohibited to the defective priest. His access to the holiest spaces is restricted even though his priesthood continues. The spatial restriction mirrors the graduated holiness structure of the entire sanctuary complex.
24: Vayedaber Moshe el-Aharon veel-banav veel-kol-benei Yisrael: Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel. The priestly holiness laws of ch. 21 are communicated to the whole people — not kept as arcane priestly knowledge but made public. The community's understanding of priestly standards matters because the community depends on and has a stake in the integrity of its mediators. The chapter closes with the widest possible audience for the priestly holiness code.
